Added ال and merged letters to make it specific to the One True God. The shadda shows emphasis.
Added kasra at the end to show that Allah owns or possesses the bounty mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters together to form the base word
Added يُ at the start to make it mean 'he gives'. The middle letters changed to make it easier to say.
Added هِ at the end to show what receives the action. This word is connected to يَشَآءُ (he wills) in the ayah, showing what Allah wills to give.

This is a special word that refers to people in a general way. Here it means 'whoever' because it's talking about anyone Allah chooses to give His favor to. It's connected to the verb يَشَآءُ (He wills/wants) that comes right after it.
Added fatha marks and the middle weak letter ي changes to ا (alif) because this is a hollow verb

steps.m: he wills/wants (subject of يُؤْتِيهِ - He gives it)
steps.n: Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and ُ at end to show he is doing the action. The long alif (آ) is because this is a hollow verb.
This is the proper name of God in Arabic. It comes from the root meaning of divinity and worship, but as a proper name it refers specifically to the One God.
Added shadda on ل to show it is doubled in pronunciation
The ُ (damma) at the end shows that Allah is the subject doing the action in this part of the sentence
Added وَ at the beginning to mean 'and'. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the first letter ا when it comes after وَ.
This is a special word that doesn't come from a typical root. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic.
Added damma (ُ) to show this is the subject form. In this ayah, it describes Allah as the possessor of great bounty.

steps.m: to give extra or do more than required

steps.n: Changes from verb (to give extra) to noun (extra/favor)
Added ال to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to Allah in the phrase 'Allah's favor'

steps.m: to be great/magnificent in size, rank, or status

steps.n: The ي between the second and third root letters makes this an intensive adjective, showing Allah's greatness is complete and perfect
ال means 'the' and makes it a specific title, while the ending ِ shows it describes Allah
